title: The Permission Slip
blurb: The White House is now vetting who gets access to frontier models before companies can share them; Moonshot's open 2.8-trillion-parameter behemoth matches US leaders; and a new paper shows that memory speed is the difference between a tool and a mind.

title: What each layer chose to remember
blurb: China's Moonshot AI released the largest open-source model in history — a 2.8-trillion-parameter system rivalling the best closed US models — built on a clever architecture trick that lets each layer decide which earlier layers to pay attention to.

title: No Honor Roll
blurb: Not one frontier AI lab earns above C-plus on the first comprehensive safety audit; an open model that can mathematically prove software is correct just found five bugs nobody knew were there; and the World AI Conference opens in Shanghai tomorrow.

title: First, the grid
blurb: New York became the first US state to freeze new hyperscale data-center permits; TSMC posted all-time chip revenue records; and a paper shows that AI training methods may be quietly optimizing the wrong thing.

title: Signals from Shanghai
blurb: Xi Jinping keynotes China's flagship AI summit for the first time; Helsing raises Europe's largest-ever defense tech round; and a Shenzhen lab runs a cancer-diagnosing neural network on a loop of optical fiber at 1/246th the energy of a GPU.

title: What they brought with them
blurb: Apple's blockbuster lawsuit against OpenAI alleges 400+ engineers carried hardware secrets out the door; NVIDIA's TwoTower paper shows how to make a language model 2.4× faster without rebuilding it; and China's GLM-5.2 matches frontier US models at a sixth of the price.

title: The still point
blurb: Google replaced its ten blue links with AI-generated summaries this week; OpenAI folded its coding agent into the main ChatGPT app; and a paper from ETH Zürich shows that a model looping until its answers stop changing can outperform rivals twice its size.

title: The structure of safety
blurb: As ICML 2026 closes in Seoul, its best position paper award goes to a finding that unsettles the field: the tools built to make AI models safe and the tools needed to make them censored are technically the same thing.

title: The review that had to come first
blurb: OpenAI's GPT-5.6 became the first model to clear a thirty-day government pre-release review — launching to the public on July 9 — while a new paper proposes storing your AI assistant's memory of you as runnable Python code.

title: The practice and the performance
blurb: OpenAI launches three new models today — Sol, Terra, and Luna — while the week's most-read research paper finds that reinforcement-learning training may have been quietly optimizing a version of every language model that never actually ships.

title: The path of least resistance
blurb: The year's top machine-learning prize went to a team who proved that giving a language model more freedom makes it think worse — and the world's most capable publicly-available chatbot left every subscription plan and started billing by the word.

title: The copy that runs
blurb: OpenAI's voice API got 25% faster; the White House is days away from formalizing frontier AI release rules; and a new paper found that every RL-trained reasoning model quietly optimizes the wrong version of itself.

title: What fewer choices unlock
blurb: The fastest frontier model in history is arriving at 750 tokens per second on Cerebras hardware — and this week's top machine-learning paper found that giving AI more freedom to think makes it think worse.

title: What was always overhead
blurb: OpenAI offers the US government a 5% equity stake worth $42 billion — while ICML 2026 opens in Seoul and an oral paper proves the optimizer behind every reasoning model was never needed for its most important training step.

title: Cooked from scratch
blurb: Meituan — a Chinese food-delivery company — secretly ran a world-beating AI model under an alias for months, then revealed it was trained entirely on domestic chips and gave it away under an open licence.

title: The wrench, not the engine
blurb: Claude's most capable model came back online after a 19-day government-enforced shutdown; a biology paper shows that AI accuracy can jump from 16.9% to 92.8% with no change to the model — just better plumbing.

title: Conditions of release
blurb: Three weeks after the US government forced the most capable model in the world offline over a security flaw, it returned — under conditions that sketch how powerful AI will actually be governed.

title: Longer, not larger
blurb: A 35-billion-parameter model matched trillion-parameter rivals by thinking in far longer chains — while Anthropic's new default model brought near-flagship capability to anyone with a free account.

title: The city you never named
blurb: A Berkeley study found that injecting your location into a hidden system prompt — even writing 'Unknown' — bends a language model's answers toward that place, with leakage up to 793 times the baseline rate.

title: What the doubter found
blurb: The world's most capable language model launched available only to twenty government-approved companies — while a physics lab proved that an AI scientist arguing with its own hypotheses before running a single experiment gets better science out the other side.

title: What thinking can't do alone
blurb: Four of the architects of Gemini and AlphaFold left Google for its rivals this week — while a new ICML paper proved there is a precise mathematical ceiling on chain-of-thought reasoning, and past that ceiling, no amount of extra thought will help.

title: The weight of a single sentence
blurb: Researchers find that when a language model writes out its thinking, only a handful of sentences — pivot points called thought anchors — actually determine the answer. Meanwhile, OpenAI's strongest model yet launches under a government-coordinated access scheme unprecedented for a commercial AI.

title: Twenty-eight million questions
blurb: Anthropic says operators linked to Alibaba's AI unit ran 25,000 fake accounts and generated 28.8 million conversations with Claude — not for answers, but to copy its capabilities into a rival model.

title: The agreeable machine
blurb: Forty-two state attorneys general subpoenaed OpenAI four days after its IPO filing — partly over evidence that ChatGPT was designed to agree with users rather than tell them the truth.

title: The shorthand no one learned
blurb: Google launched its most capable public reasoning model yet on Sunday, while a new paper shows AI systems can read a compressed notation — unreadable to any human — and lose almost none of the meaning.

title: The architecture of thought
blurb: The co-inventor of the Transformer — the framework beneath every major AI system — just left Google for OpenAI, as a new paper by the man who invented LSTM asks whether AI models are wasting their compute by thinking out loud.

title: Six million lessons
blurb: Six million GitHub pull requests, one fine-tuned model, a nearly-tripled score — and three words that prompted the US government to pull Anthropic's most capable models offline globally.

title: A short note in the margin
blurb: Cornell researchers proved that adding roughly thirteen words to a Reddit comment can silently steer AI deep-research tools toward fake restaurants and invented businesses — exposing a structural flaw in how every AI research agent reads the web.

title: What the mind holds constant
blurb: Waymo recalled 3,871 robotaxis after its software drove into closed construction zones — and a PNAS study finds the same gap everywhere: machines can orient attention, but cannot hold a rule constant against conflict.

title: The whole stack
blurb: SpaceX paid sixty billion dollars for the coding tool developers open every morning — and launched a 1.5-trillion-parameter model trained on real developer workflows to run inside it.

title: The dress rehearsal
blurb: Before last week's model upgrade reached users, OpenAI had already run a million real conversations through it — while Anthropic's security model found 10,000 critical flaws in power grids and hospitals across 15 countries.

title: The mark before the grade
blurb: A new paper solves reinforcement learning's open-ended grading problem by teaching AI to build its own answer key — while the White House signs the first formal framework for inspecting frontier models before they ship.

title: Three days, then a directive
blurb: Anthropic's two most powerful new models launched Monday, earned the highest benchmark marks ever seen, and were pulled offline by US government order on Thursday — the first time a frontier model has been forced off the market this way.

title: What the hand no longer writes
blurb: More than 80% of new code shipping at one of the world's leading labs was written by its own model — while a Penn Engineering paper borrows a 1940s maths trick to make physics AI 6–10 times more efficient.

title: Everything at once
blurb: Google's DiffusionGemma rewrites how text is generated — starting from noise and resolving all 256 words in parallel, four times faster — while Anthropic's Fable 5 quietly crossed the quality threshold once considered too sensitive to release.

title: The recipe, at last
blurb: Princeton ran 300 experiments to unlock the recipe for world-class AI image generation — and published every answer, as OpenAI quietly filed to go public at $852 billion.

title: What a model holds in mind
blurb: Google's TurboQuant paper compresses AI's working memory up to six times without losing a single answer — the same week Microsoft shipped seven AI models it built entirely without OpenAI.

title: The reader who never tires
blurb: At AAAI 2026, a machine reviewed 22,977 research papers overnight and most scientists preferred it to their human reviewers — the same week Anthropic released its most powerful model yet.

title: The physics they taught a machine to feel
blurb: A British startup raised $300 million to replace days-long engineering simulations with deep learning — and a Meta paper found that compressing a reasoning model makes it talk itself out of answers it had already found.

title: Twenty-six minutes of thought
blurb: Apple licensed Siri's brain from Google for $1 billion a year at Tim Cook's last WWDC — and a Harvard-Perplexity study showed that autonomous agents don't just speed up work, they shift what work you attempt.

title: The art of seeing without being taught
blurb: Meta's new vision model trained on 1.7 billion unlabeled photos and outperformed specialists — as Anthropic filed to go public near a trillion-dollar valuation.

title: A polite word in the wrong ear
blurb: A study in Nature Communications finds that the most capable reasoning models can talk other AI models into crossing safety lines — at a 97% success rate, with no human in the loop — just as Congress proposes its most ambitious federal AI law yet.

title: Before the states could disagree
blurb: Congress drafts a bill to override fifty state AI laws with one federal standard for three years — and a new paper quietly gives AI models a better working memory.

title: All that deliberation, and yet
blurb: A study finds reasoning models write deep plans but pick moves on first impressions alone — the week Microsoft unveiled its first AI built entirely without OpenAI.

title: Not every job needs the big machine
blurb: Anthropic files to go public at a valuation near a trillion dollars, while a new NVIDIA paper argues the most capable part of an AI agent system might be its cheapest piece.

title: Learning what's worth your time
blurb: A paper teaches software to spot great research ideas using citation patterns — and the biggest AI coding tool starts charging by the session.

title: One move at a time
blurb: A new study finds language models appear to plan deeply but actually decide shallowly — just as a billion dollars lands on the startup whose AI writes 89% of its own code.

title: The mapmakers who keep drawing the same map
blurb: A new study finds AI research agents cluster around familiar ideas rather than explore new ones — right as a British safety test crowns the first model to cross the autonomous-attack threshold.

title: Eighty years, one question, one night
blurb: An AI model cracks an 80-year geometry problem using tools from an entirely different branch of maths, and the experts who checked it were surprised.

title: When software started managing itself
blurb: A new model that runs a hundred helpers at once, Google quietly rewiring how the planet searches, and the word everyone suddenly says: harness.
